data.title: W.P. Carey Is Fairly Valued Among Net Lease Peers
data.description:
The purpose of today’s article is to dive into the real estate life cycle and explore change within the net lease sector. Over the past three years, I have covered the net lease sector in depth, dissecting the challenges facing the business model of long leased assets. More specifically, a large portion of my coverage has been related to the dramatic effects of rising interest rates on long duration real assets, such as single tenant net lease.

Perhaps my best summary of the challenges facing the broader sector came in my article titled “Rates Up, REITs Down! It's Opportunity Cost...”. I would recommend giving this article a read if you are new to net lease. At the least, my thesis is below:

When the risk-free rate increases, it causes yields across other asset classes to rise. Opportunity cost dictates that this is true. This is because the relative risk of the income is being repriced based on its relationship with the ten-year treasury’s yield. After all, net lease REITs must offer some risk premium over the yield of a risk-free asset, otherwise, why would investors be taking the risk.

Movements of the ten-year treasury are one of the new causes of systemic movements in REIT stock prices. The changes in treasury yields dictate short-term decision making for investors looking to ensure that they maintain a balanced risk posture in the marketplace. This means the now is driving decisions instead of information around the future.

Today, we are going to dive even deeper into this theme. More specifically, we are going to revisit W.P. Carey (NYSE:WPC), one of the largest net lease REITs and examine what a “new normal” looks like after several tumultuous years. I will review the company’s portfolio and provide thoughts on whether the company’s stock offers value today. Before reviewing WPC’s current portfolio and revisiting my prior thesis, we should review my coverage over the past twelve months as there is a compelling story to be told.

Review Of Prior Coverage

As one of the largest net lease REITs, WPC has received a lot of attention on Seeking Alpha. Over the past year, I have covered the REIT on two occasions, offering my thoughts on the company’s valuation at two distinct times. Backing up even further, my initial coverage of WPC was in 2021, before significant changes took place to the company’s business.

My initial article on WPC was titled “Rising Income Supported By Mission Critical Real Estate”. In this article, I provided an initial overview of WPC’s business including their history of pioneering sale leasebacks across the pond in Europe. Over time, the REIT had grown to become one of the most prominent players in European net lease real estate. However, shortly thereafter, the company began to run into trouble. This is where the excitement began for WPC.

The past four years have been an exciting time for WPC investors. Perhaps exciting is not exactly the right word as the company went through one of the most tumultuous periods in their multi-decade operating history. To understand why, we must examine the value proposition to shareholders. Historically, one of the main points of attraction for WPC was their dividend policy. For more than 10 years, the company maintained a policy of every dividend larger than the last, incrementally marching upward, quarter after quarter. For investors, this offered extraordinary value as shares of the company were paying a relatively high yield, at least compared to the 10 year treasury, and the payments were growing quarterly at a rate alongside long-term inflation.

From inside of the company, this value proposition was fueled by a multi-cylinder approach including internal growth from embedded rent escalations, external growth from the company's acquisition heavy strategy, and other ancillary business units including their third-party asset management wing. Companies operating in net lease typically have a variety of levers to pull. Some companies lean heavily into the development of new assets to generate additional yield, while others add additional businesses such as asset management arms to try and enhance their scale.

For some time, this strategy of every dividend larger than the last worked. The dividend continued to March upward appreciating at a long-term growth rate of around 2% annually.

That is, until the math began to catch up for the company. You see, alongside the rising dividend of WPC came a rising dividend payout ratio. Over time, the company began distributing a larger and larger portion of earnings. This situation was manageable until the effects of rising interest rates began to take hold. Roughly two years ago, the company was forced to take action including a dividend cut and the spin off of Net Lease Office Properties (NLOP). When WPC ran out of rope for their dividend amid rising tenant bankruptcies and rising financing costs, the company initiated a portfolio overhaul to make significant changes.

In an article published in 2024, I examined these changes in detail.

W. P. Carey has made significant changes to its portfolio, including a spinoff of office assets, the repurchase of self-storage assets by U-Haul, and the disposition of hotel properties.

While the company was overhauling their portfolio to increase the portion invested in industrial assets and remove non-core properties. The company also right-sized their quarterly distribution. To the chagrin of investors, the dividend was cut from $1.071 to $0.86 per share per quarter, a reduction of around 30%.

At this point, WPC appeared to establish a new normal with a better payout ratio, a better portfolio, and better outlook. But, the loss of confidence from the market meant WPC was trapped deep in the doldrums, trading at a significant discount to sector peers. In an incisive article, I explained why the company’s discounted valuation was attractive. I explored why the company looked appealing despite tenant bankruptcies. Since publication, WPC shares have appreciated by around 15%.

Following a significant repricing and several action packed years, WPC shares have stabilized. In fact, the price of the stock over the past six months, setting aside the tariff madness, shows that relative stability has returned to WPC and net lease more broadly.

With that in mind, let’s recap where WPC stands today.

REIT Overview

While much has changed for WPC over the past several years, the company is still a single tenant net lease REIT that focuses on a core mission of high quality industrial properties around the United States and Western Europe. Currently, the property owns 1,614 net lease properties covering 177,000,000 square feet. In addition, the company owns 78 self storage operating properties. The company's annualized base rent totals $1.4 billion, and roughly 2/3 of assets are invested in North America.

Roughly 25% of rent is generated from tenants with an investment grade credit rating, and the portfolio is around 98% occupied. The company's weighted average lease term remaining is 12.3 years, and less than 10% of the annualized base rent is generated from the top ten tenants.

The largest tenant of WPC is Extra Space Storage (EXR), occupying 39 properties and generating $38 million in annualized base rent, accounting for 2.7% of total rental revenue. Interestingly, most of the remaining top ten tenants are foreign.

One might ask themselves what a net lease REIT is doing with another REIT as their top tenant. Allow me to explain…

There are a variety of ways to skin the cat across the real estate game. Looking at WPC and EXR, these two companies both operate inside of the real estate world, but with two very different business models. WPC is a long-term investor, buying assets encumbered by long lease terms. In contrast, EXR is a real estate operating business, leasing self storage units to customers on a short-term basis. As a form of leverage, EXR may sell their real estate to another REIT like WPC, opting to offload the property from their balance sheet. This means EXR will pay rent to WPC while the company collects the spread generated by their asset-level operating activities. Spread investing comes in many shapes and sizes.

The company has an interesting focus when it comes to embedded rent escalations. Roughly half of the company's leases feature CPI linked rental increases. The remainder have fixed increases. For many investors, it was the belief that these CPI linked leases would supercharge the company during the inflationary post pandemic era. What we quickly learned was that many of these CPI linked leases are capped somewhere around 3%, leaving limited upside if inflation spikes materially, as was the case.

Right now, the company's portfolio is split primarily across industrial, warehouse, retail, and other properties. The company combines industrial to a total of 63% of the portfolio but differentiates between warehouse/distribution space and manufacturing. The larger portion of the company's industrial allocation is in specialized manufacturing facilities which may be less fungible than warehouses. This means the industrial assets are not comparable to those owned by STAG Industrial (STAG) or a higher quality industrial operator.

WPC benefits from a quality balance sheet that is enhanced by their investment grade credit ratings. Currently, the balance sheet is capitalized with 63% equity, and the remaining portion allocated to debt. Debt covers just over 1/3 of total gross assets, and most is senior unsecured notes. Net debt to Adjusted EBITDA is 5.8x, relatively low in the net lease sector. The company's weighted average debt maturity is just inside of five years.

The company is facing debt that will need to be addressed over the next couple of years. 20% of total debt expires in 2026 and 2027, with a combined weighted average interest rate of under 3%. While this debt is split between the United States and Europe, the reality is that WPC will likely be refinancing into higher financing costs as interest rates remain higher for longer and the company's debt maturities are approaching. Looking through the end of the decade, around 50% of the company's debt will mature.

The all important for many folks is the WPC company’s dividend payment. Following their cut towards the end of 2023, the company has once again returned to their popular strategy of “every dividend larger than the last”. After resetting the dividend to $0.86 per share, the company has begun marching the payout upwards once again. Most recently, the payout was increased from $0.89 to $0.90 per share, a modest increase of 1.1%, which annualized to 4.4%

Where’s The Beef?

From my perspective, the company has completed a transformation which has once again repositioned the company into an attractive net lease REIT. Following several tumultuous years, the business appears to have stabilized as the company offloads non-core assets, improves their tenant exposures, and addresses key operational headwinds. While the company experienced a hurricane throughout 2023 and 2024, the relative stability of 2025 has been displayed through the company's stock price.

However, the reality is that this puts the company in line with other high-quality businesses currently trading in the sector.

Below is a recently updated version of my net lease matrix showing key valuation considerations for net lease REITs.

Above is an illustration of the company's valuation against major competitors within net lease. As you can see, WPC is currently trading at an adjusted funds from operations per share multiple of 13 times. The sector low is currently Broadstone Net Lease (BNL) trading at just 10.8 times AFFO per share. Situated on the other end of the spectrum is Agree Realty (ADC), which continues to trade at the highest valuation multiple in the sector at above 17 times adjusted funds from operations per share. Looking at payout ratio, WPC is currently distributing around 73% of earnings, around the midpoint of net lease and aligned with Realty Income (O).

Right now, I see WPC as being fairly priced in the middle of the net lease pack. The company has not earned the premium valuation of high quality REITs like ADC due to their ongoing tenant issues. The company has also failed to earn the premium assigned to fast growing REITs like EPRT with a payout ratio below two thirds of AFFO. Instead, Mr. Market has elected to price WPC alongside other large, mature REITs such as O.

Where shares are trading, I see WPC as being priced fairly. It does not look as expensive as a REIT like Netstreit (NTST) which is trading at a premium valuation despite a weak portfolio. Only time will tell if management can actively reshape the portfolio and capitalize on their low payout ratio to do so. On the other hand, WPC does not trade as cheaply as BNL, which is experiencing similar tenant woes, but has a materially higher payout ratio of almost 80%. In theory, these assets are priced efficiently as the market digests their corresponding strengths and weaknesses.

WPC is likely situated in the middle of the pack because the market continues to digest both good and bad news for the company. The balance sheet and portfolio look much better nearly two years after the spinoff of NLOP. However, there are still ongoing tenant issues as WPC failed to see several key bankruptcies coming.

Conclusion & Risks

WPC is scheduled to report Q2 earnings next week. This earnings report will provide an important update on key metrics and portfolio health. Following the prior quarter’s earnings where WPC’s management team noted healthy acquisition activity alongside tenant headwinds, this quarter will provide similar key updates. Personally, I am going to continue to monitor key issues around Hellweg, a top ten tenant that is restructuring leases. Below is an excerpt from the prior quarter’s earnings call.

Hellweg's status is also largely unchanged since our recent press release. It remains current on rent, although it continues to face a challenging operating environment, including weak German consumer spending and a competitive do-it-yourself industry. Hellweg continues to work with its key stakeholders, including landlords and lenders to further improve its liquidity and those conversations are ongoing.

In the meantime, we're actively reducing our exposure, executing agreements at the end of March to take back 12 stores, representing about 1/3 of our total exposure, with 7 stores terminated by September of this year and another 5 stores by September of next year. We expect to re-tenant most of those stores, achieving rents in line with their existing rents and to sell the rest. In both cases, with limited downtime.

Those steps should help improve Hellweg's, while also giving us clear line of sight to moving Hellweg out of our top 10 tenants.

I will be keeping a keen eye on any updates from this particular tenant. Additionally, I'm going to be looking for other updates from key tenant issues including True Value and Hearthside. These three tenant issues colliding at once are illustrative examples of the company's failure to get ahead of the game.

Despite requiring tenant financial reporting, the company's credit research team failed to actively position themselves ahead of issues. In upcoming earnings calls, I will be more weary to “take management's word for it” when it comes to tenant health.

I see WPC as being fairly priced. Currently trading at a dividend yield and AFFO yield of 5.6% and 7.7%, respectively, WPC has once again been valued in the middle of the net lease pack. While companies ebb and flow, market efficiency generally brings each company back to where they belong. At this point, WPC is no longer valued as attractively as when the market had the company in the penalty box. In contrast, the AFFO multiple is far behind the top performers from an operational perspective, making WPC a Hold.

data.title: GameSquare: Monetizing Attention, But Still Early To Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It’s not GameSquare’s (NASDAQ:GAME) moment, at least not yet. But it’s not a far-fetched idea either.

Since integrating FaZe Clan, the company has started to build a real operational business. They’ve cut costs, the advertising unit continues to gain traction, and their Ethereum exposure adds an edge few others in the sector can offer.

But that’s where the questions begin. Margins are still not consolidated, monetization depends on third parties, and there are no clear KPIs to track the execution roadmap.

At the current price of $1.33, the stock fairly reflects what’s working today. The sum-of-the-parts valuation points to some undervaluation, and the market still isn’t pricing in the potential future upside if the company delivers on its guidance.

Given the uncertainty still surrounding GAME, the current multiples seem reasonable.

That’s why my thesis is clear. Hold for those already in the position, and wait to see if management can turn promises into results.

Overview

GameSquare is a company that sells attention. And as we all know, that pays well these days.

Since acquiring FaZe Clan in March 2024, GameSquare has transformed into an ecosystem built to reach massive audiences, build community, and monetize it through content creation.

The idea is simple. If you capture attention, you can capture monetization. And for that, GAME is building a strategy based on three business verticals.

The first is its advertising and sponsorships unit. This is the company’s spearhead. With agencies like Cut+Sew or GCN, they design campaigns for giants like Nike, Pepsi, or Adidas, integrating influencers into esports tournaments, livestreams, or in-person events.

Then comes its esports and entertainment business. With FaZe Clan and Complexity, they have direct access to top gaming leagues and tournaments. And in terms of audience, they combine for over 17 million followers across Twitch and YouTube.

The third unit is talent representation. GameSquare works with over 100 content creators who, together, surpass one billion followers across platforms and generate over 2.5 billion impressions per quarter.

And all of this is produced, distributed, and monetized in-house. No intermediaries eating into margins.

The market is there, and it’s growing every year. The question is whether they can turn it into a profitable business beyond follows and likes.

What’s Next?

It’s time to start laying out the path that management is proposing for GameSquare’s future. The integration of FaZe Clan was the first move. In March 2024, that process was completed, and they began working on reducing structural costs and balancing the books. And it seems to be working. In Q1 2025, they cut SG&A expenses by 24%, and they expect to reach break-even on adjusted EBITDA before year-end.

From here, the next step is to continue scaling their advertising business. This unit accounts for 62% of total revenue, and starting in 2025, the plan is to increase campaign volume by 20%.

That said, it doesn’t look like a sustainable pace. A big part of that growth will come from the recent FaZe merger, and the company itself admits it won’t be able to maintain that acceleration every year.

The next focus is content distribution. Right now, only 28% of their sponsored content runs on their own Twitch, YouTube, and TikTok channels, the rest depends on third parties. The goal is to raise that figure to 40% over the next 24 months to improve margins.

But beyond mentioning their in-house content creators and a few internal campaign tracking tools, there aren’t many details on how they plan to do it.

GameSquare claims it wants to build a profitable long-term model. They’re aiming for 15% annual revenue growth and a 10% adjusted EBITDA margin starting in 2027.

But that’s all on paper. It’s still unclear whether they’ll have the operational capacity to deliver. So far, the FaZe integration shows positive signs, but the growth plan lacks specific KPIs to track execution.

It’s a reasonable roadmap in my view, but without concrete KPIs and with a business model still under construction, it’s hard to know if this is a projection or just a PowerPoint.

For now, the FaZe integration looks solid, so there’s no reason to distrust the guidance. At least for now.

What Is GameSquare Worth Today?

The best way to value GameSquare right now is to break it down into parts. First, its spearhead: Advertising and Sponsorships. This unit is generating over $37 million annually, with estimated growth between 13% and 15%. If we apply the sector’s conservative multiples between 2.2x and 2.5x sales, its value would be somewhere between $80 and $95 million. Not much mystery there.

Then we have the Esports & Entertainment business. According to guidance, it’s expected to generate around $15 million in 2025. But this segment is harder to monetize. Fixed and operating costs are high, audience doesn’t always translate to revenue, and the tournaments and platforms are owned by third parties (Valve, Riot, Activision).

That’s why assigning a multiple of 1.5–2.0x feels like the conservative move. Even so, it justifies a reasonable range between $22 and $30 million.

As for cash, they hold $4.5 million net. No meaningful financial debt. But the most interesting part sits outside the traditional model. GameSquare holds a position in Ethereum worth roughly $38 million.

This impacts the balance sheet and it’s also part of a plan to generate cash flow.

The company wants to use Ethereum to generate yield by lending it through decentralized platforms (DEFI) or participating in liquidity pools. Their own guidance projects an annual return between 6% and 10%, which translates to $2.3–3.8 million in additional revenue.

Then there’s FaZe Clan. I’m not including it under Esports & Entertainment because it operates more like a brand asset. Its book value after the acquisition was set at $38.6 million, but given the doubts around its direct monetization, I prefer to apply a more conservative range between $14 and $17 million as an intangible asset.

If we add everything up, the result lands between $158 and $185 million. And considering its current market cap of $140 million, you could argue that the stock trades with a reasonable margin of safety.

What Could GameSquare Be Worth Tomorrow?

My view is that if GameSquare executes on its guidance, the three-year upside isn’t crazy. But, as always, it all comes down to execution.

What’s already working is Advertising & Sponsorships, which currently brings in $37 million. If it maintains a 13% annual growth rate and manages to increase the share of content distributed through its own channels from 28% to 40%, this unit could reach $55 to $65 million in revenue. Using similar conservative multiples as today, that would imply a valuation between $120 and $160 million.

Just with that unit, GameSquare could already justify most of its future market cap.

Then there’s Esports & Entertainment. If they manage to improve monetization and stabilize operations, it could generate $20 to $23 million annually. Applying sector-adjusted multiples, that would put its value between $30 and $45 million.

The gaming ecosystem keeps growing. Audience numbers rose 9%, prize pools surpassed $350 million, and over $1.2 billion has been poured into sponsorships. There’s no reason to think that level of revenue is out of reach.

The Ethereum position is also relevant in this valuation. If they execute their DeFi strategy properly and the projected returns hold, that part alone could reasonably be worth between $50 and $58 million.

Not to mention its potential revaluation in the market.

The big question mark is still FaZe. If they manage to stabilize the brand and monetize it through licensing, merchandise, or exclusive content, assigning a range of $20 to $25 million as a brand asset doesn’t seem far-fetched.

With all of that on the table, GameSquare’s potential valuation could land between $224 million and $293 million over the next three years.

At that level, GameSquare stock would be trading between $2.46 and $3.22 by 2028—up from $1.33 at the time I’m writing this. That implies a potential upside between +60% and +110%, equivalent to an annual CAGR of 17% to 28%. If we apply a 12% discount rate to that valuation range to account for execution risk, dilution, and uncertainty around FaZe, the present value of that upside would land between $1.75 and $2.25 per share. There are still plenty of questions, but I’m inclined to bet on this path—because, so far, management hasn’t given me any reason to think otherwise. This is a thesis that’s finally starting to rest on something tangible.

The Bottom Line

GameSquare isn’t an obvious bet. And that’s why I’m leaning toward assigning a hold rating to the stock. It’s a small cap with margins still under construction, in a sector that attracts more viewers than profits. But it’s also a company that’s starting to get things right.

It successfully integrated FaZe. It cut costs. And it’s starting to build an operational structure that simply didn’t exist before.

In the short term, the current price reflects the sum-of-the-parts fairly well. We’re not in buy territory—but not in sell territory either. However, if we look out over the next three years, the thesis starts to look more appealing.

The advertising business is growing at a good pace. Ethereum exposure could bring upside and additional income. And the possibility of monetizing FaZe as a brand—while still a challenge—isn’t off the table.

If they execute the plan well, GameSquare’s potential valuation would sit between $224 and $293 million. That implies a share price between $2.46 and $3.22 in three years—versus $1.33 at the time of writing.

That’s a potential upside of +60% to +110%, with an estimated CAGR of 17% to 28% per year. Looking at the numbers, I don’t think they need to execute everything perfectly for GAME to be worth more than it is today. This isn’t a hyper-growth stock—but it is an opportunity with some margin of safety and clear catalysts.

That said—if you’re hunting for a multibagger, this probably isn’t your game.

data.title: Crescent Energy Is Assembling Operations
data.description:
Crescent Energy Company (NYSE:CRGY), an Eagle Ford and Uinta Basin oil and gas producer, has grown through acquisition, notably of SilverBow Resources. It is the third largest gross oil and gross gas producer in the south Texas Eagle Ford.

In April, the company simplified its equity structure, converting all private Class B shares to public Class A shares; it still has convertible equity outstanding. Investment firm KKR has a 10% ownership interest and manages the company.

The South Texas Eagle Ford play is a complex mix of black oil, condensate, natural gas liquids, and natural gas. However, the Eagle Ford is advantaged versus the West Texas Permian by more sufficient gas and liquids takeaway capacity.

With a net loss in the first quarter due to non-cash costs of depreciation and a gain on derivatives, the company’s stock is at a low point in its 52-week range. It offers a dividend of 5.2%.

I own shares but am ranking this as a hold for investors seeking capital appreciation due to the company’s high debt costs and the potential for share dilution.

The company’s second quarter earnings call is scheduled for Tuesday, August 5, 2025.

Acquisition of SilverBow Resources and Ridgemar Energy's Eagle Ford Assets

Crescent Energy’s acquisition of SilverBow Resources (primarily south Texas Eagle Ford assets and operations) closed a year ago on July 30, 2024. The total deal value (including debt acquisition) was $2.1 billion. Per Crescent's announcement: “SilverBow shareholders elected to receive in aggregate approximately $358 million in total cash consideration as part of the transaction. Crescent issued approximately 52 million shares of Class A common stock to fund the non-cash portion of the consideration. As of closing, former SilverBow shareholders own approximately 23% of the combined company on a fully diluted basis.”

On January 31, 2025, Crescent closed the acquisition of Eagle Ford assets from private company Ridgemar Energy for $905 million: $830 million in cash and approximately 5.5 million shares of Class A common stock, plus future oil price contingent consideration.

Of note, Ridgemar had acquired at least some of those assets a year earlier, in May 2023, for $700 million from Callon Petroleum.

Operations

The company has 540,000 acres in the Eagle Ford and 1465 gross locations. In the Uinta, it has 145,000 acres and 650 gross locations. The Uinta is a small basin but leans toward heavy, sweet oil; the Eagle Ford has complex strata of gas, oil, and liquids.

Crescent also has some low-decline-rate conventional production in Wyoming, where it is applying enhanced oil recovery and potentially carbon capture technologies.

Macro

With a major industrial and tech focus on building artificial intelligence data centers, and generating the additional baseload electricity they require, companies and utilities have turned to fuels like natural gas for the immediate term and nuclear for the long-term. Some deals, as with the natural gas-plus-nuclear like that just announced by Liberty Energy (LBRT) and Oklo (OKLO) use both.

Moreover, the current administration’s encouragement and support for building more LNG export terminals has also boosted demand for natural gas.

Oil & Gas Prices

The mid-day July 24, 2025, price for West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude oil at Cushing, Oklahoma for September 2025 delivery was $65.44/barrel. Natural gas price for August 2025 delivery at Henry Hub, Louisiana was $3.09/million British Thermal Units (MMBTU).

From the July 8, 2025, Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO) by the US Energy Information Administration (EIA), the 5-95 confidence interval for WTI oil at year-end 2026 is about $35-$115/barrel with a slight downward trend for the average forecasted and NYMEX futures prices. The 5-95 confidence interval for Henry Hub gas at year-end 2026 is $2-$11/MMBTU with an upward trend for the average forecasted and NYMEX futures prices.

The Dutch Title Transfer Facility (TTF) price for August 2025 LNG was $11.27/MMBTU.

First Quarter 2025 Results and Guidance

For the first quarter of 2025, Crescent Energy had average sales of 258,000 BOE/D, about 60% liquids (102,000 BPD of oil and 47,000 BPD of natural gas liquids), with the remaining 40% natural gas. The average price, after derivatives, was $38.93/BOE, or $67.17/bbl for oil, $25.13/bbl for NGLs, and $3.09/MCF for natural gas.

Crescent’s Q1 2025 net loss attributable to Crescent Energy was -$2.15 million, or -$0.01/share. Quarterly net income was $5.9 million; however, subtractions of $2.0 million and $6.1 million were made for income owed to, respectively, noncontrolling interests and noncontrolling redeemable interests.

Net cash from Q1 2025 operations was $337 million, including noncash addbacks of $282 million for DD&A (depreciation, depletion, and amortization) and $91 million mark-to-market loss on derivatives.

For the second quarter of 2025, the company expects oil production to increase due to a full quarter of owning the Ridgemar assets. Also, the company is more hedged than most to protect its balance sheet and capital investments.

Reserves

The table below from its most recent 10-K shows Crescent Energy reserves at year-end 2024, so this includes SilverBow Resources but does not include the Ridgemar acquisition. The PV-10 reserve value at that time was $6.3 billion. Net proved reserves were about 698 million barrels of oil equivalent, 63% liquids (42% oil and 21% plant products, or natural gas liquids).

Competitors

Crescent Energy is headquartered in Houston, Texas.

Because it operates in south Texas Eagle Ford, the Utah Uinta, and the Wyoming Rockies, Crescent Energy competes with companies in the Bakken, the Rockies, the Uinta and throughout Texas (Permian as well as Eagle Ford), and Louisiana (Haynesville).

In the Uinta, Crescent Energy competes with SM Energy (SM), Berry Corporation (BRY), and several private companies.

In the Eagle Ford (south Texas) specifically, Crescent competes with: BP (BP), Baytex (BTE), ConocoPhillips (COP) (through its acquisition of Marathon Oil), Chevron (CVX), Devon Energy (DVN), EOG (EOG), INEOS, Magnolia Oil and Gas (MGY), Permian Resources (PR) through its acquisition of Earthstone Energy, SM Energy, Exxon Mobil (XOM), and other private and international companies.

Governance

At June 30, 2025, 7.8% of Crescent’s floated stock was shorted. Insiders own 4.2% of the floated stock and institutions own 73.01% of the floated stock. The company’s beta is somewhat above overall market volatility at 1.23, reasonable for an oil and gas exploration and production company.

Potential investors should be aware that KKR owns 10% of Crescent Energy and manages it. Per Crescent's most recent 10-K, “We are a party to a Management Agreement that engages the Manager to provide certain management and investment advisory services to us and our subsidiaries. The Manager is an indirect subsidiary of the KKR Group. The KKR Group is a leading global investment firm that offers alternative asset management as well as capital markets and insurance solutions. Pursuant to the Management Agreement, the Manager has agreed to provide us with management services, including our senior executive management team, and other assistance, including with respect to strategic planning, risk management, identifying and screening potential acquisitions, identifying and analyzing sustainability-related issues, and providing such other assistance as we may require.”

On March 31, 2025, Crescent Energy’s public Class A stock held by institutions, some of which represent index fund investments that match the overall market, included BlackRock (10.3%), Vanguard (8.1%), American Century (5.5%), State Street (3.8%), Bank of New York Mellon (3.7%), and Dimensional Fund Advisors (2.4%).

The December 31, 2024, ownership structure is shown below. However, instead of 26% Class B and 74% Class A shown in the diagram, the division as of March 31, 2025, is slightly different at 25.4% Class B shares (65.26 million) and 74.6% Class A (191.29 million) for a total of 256.5 million shares.

As noted below, all Class B shares converted to Class A shares in April 2025.

Company ownership simplified somewhat, effective April 4, 2025.*KKR still owns 10% of the company

*All Class B shares converted to Class A shares as of April 2025.

However, implied shares currently total 318.25 million, or 61.7 million shares additional to the 256.5 million shares noted above. Per the company's most recent 10-Q, these additional shares appear to mainly represent the conversion of convertible subsidiary equity for the Ridgemar acquisition (5.5 million shares) and the SilverBow Resources acquisition (51.6 million shares) into common shares.

Crescent Energy’s Financial and Stock Highlights

With a July 24, 2025, closing price of $9.28/share, Crescent Energy’s total market capitalization is $2.95 billion. Note Class A shareholder market capitalization is smaller, at $2.4 billion.

The company’s 52-week price range is $6.83-$16.94 per share, so the closing price is 55% of the one-year high. The one-year target price is $14.15/share: the closing price is 66% of that level.

Trailing twelve months’ earnings per share is negative at -$0.64/share.

In the last three quarters Crescent beat analysts’ estimates by $0.12/share to $0.38/share.

The averages of analysts estimated 2025 and 2026 earnings per share (EPS) are $1.63 and $1.54, respectively, giving the company a forward P/E ratio range of 5.7-6.0.

Trailing twelve months’ (TTM)) operating cash flow was $1.38 billion and levered free cash flow was -$715 million.

TTM return on assets and equity are unimpressive at 1.4% and -2.5%, respectively.

Crescent Energy pays a dividend of $0.48/share or 5.2%. The current 2-year Treasury yield is lower at 3.92%.

The company was recently successful in buying back about 30% ($300 million) of its $1 billion in 9.25% notes due in 2028 and in privately placing $600 million of 8.375% notes due in 2034.

At March 31, 2025, Crescent had $5.44 billion in liabilities, including $3.6 billion in long-term debt and $9.87 billion in assets giving it a liability-to-asset ratio of 55%. It also had $1.17 billion of redeemable noncontrolling interests. If this obligation is lumped together with liabilities instead of equity, the (liability+convertible)-to-asset ratio rises to 67%.

Mean analyst rating is a 2.1 or “buy” from thirteen analysts.

Notes on Valuation

The company’s book value per share of $16.70 is above its current market price, indicating negative investor sentiment.

The ratio of enterprise value to EBITDA is a low 4.1, far below the range of 10.0 or less that suggests a bargain, thus a considerable bargain by this measure.

Seeking Alpha Factor Grades are:

Valuation B+

Growth C-

Profitability D-

Momentum D-

Revisions A

Seeking Alpha Dividend Grades are:

Safety C+

Growth F

Yield B

Consistency F.

Positive and Negative Risks

Investors should consider their US oil, natural gas, and NGL price expectations as the factors most likely to affect Crescent Energy.

Political risk is positive as the current US administration is pro all energy sources, including baseload coal and natural gas for AI data centers.

Recommendations for Crescent Energy

Crescent Energy is a pastiche, with Uinta, Eagle Ford, and Wyoming reserves and production.

The company is managed by KKR, similar to other private equity portfolio companies but with a much larger public piece: KKR has only a 10% ownership stake.

While the company simplified its ownership structure by converting Class B shares to Class A shares, there is still considerable outstanding convertible equity.

Crescent’s dividend of 5.2% is above 2-year Treasury rate of 3.92%.

Although I own shares speculatively and dividend-hunters may like its 5.2% dividend rate, I rank Crescent Energy as a hold for those seeking capital appreciation due to dilution risk, high debt costs, and uncertainty of asset integration from its relatively new acquisitions in the operationally challenging Eagle Ford play.

data.title: UniCredit Q2: Record Quarter, Dividend To Watch, Caution Advised
data.description:
Banks represent one of the most astonishing narrative turnarounds we have seen in this century. After the Great Financial Crisis, they have usually traded at depressed valuations and were despised and shunned as if they were spreading mortal illnesses. The reputational damage suffered by the whole industry was substantial. Then Covid came, and the post-pandemic rebound brought along red-hot inflation. Central banks hiked interest rates, and banks thrived, being one of the best-performing sectors in the market.

UniCredit (OTCPK:UNCRY, OTCPK:UNCFF) distinguished itself even more positively from the crowd. Back in 2020, it traded as low as EUR 7 per share. You can now buy a share for EUR 60. Since my last article written in April 2024, the stock has returned a whopping 125%, 100 percentage points above the S&P500. Now, this is alpha, and it comes from one of the largest European banks, not from some unknown stock.

The big question is whether UniCredit still offers upside or not. Well, the bank just released its 1H earnings, and we have some food for thought. Let's digest it together.

Last year, I pointed out that UniCredit's success was mainly linked to a successful turnaround, partly linked to Italy's growing household gross wealth that needs to be managed, generating commission fees. On the other hand, UniCredit focused on reducing costs and generating more value under the leadership of its CEO, Mr. Orcel. Since he took the reins in 2021, UniCredit has delivered outstanding results, and it has become one of the key players in the so-called bank-consolidation-risk that is going on in Italy and in Europe.

Surely, high interest rates helped many banks and their net interest revenue. But now that the ECB is lowering rates as inflation seems tamed and the economy needs some help to combat the drawback caused by geopolitical tensions (rare earth control, gas supplies, trade tariffs, wars, etc.), we need to understand how UniCredit expects to keep posting great results.

Just a quick recap before we start. Banks are a different type of business from industrial or service companies. In a way, their product is capital itself. Moreover, they are highly leveraged and regulated. This requires us to use appropriate metrics to analyze their results.

First, we have to consider their ROE and ROA as the most important profitability metrics, together with net interest margin and the ever-so-important cost-to-income ratio. Banks are simply obsessed with this ratio to prove to investors that their operational efficiency is improving. The target is to be below 50%. Secondly, we need to assess credit quality and risk metrics. Non-performing loans represent the credit risk exposure, and we want them to be as low as possible. Sometimes, it is important to know the coverage ratio to see the cushion a bank has against credit losses. Usually, conservative banks are above 100%. Regulators require banks to have capital buffers. So, capital adequacy is measured with the CET1 ratio, which we expect to be at least 10%. Finally, while the PE ratio is useful, banks are better assessed through the price-to-book ratio, which compares the share price to the net value of their assets.

Well, UniCredit reported its 1H ever, with 2Q25 beating net profit and RoTE expectations, leading the bank to up its 2025 guidance and its 2027 ambition.

Core revenue was EUR 5.9B, +1.3% YoY. Net profit of EUR 3.3B (ex one-offs EUR 2.9B) was a record, and RoTE improved to 24.1%% for the quarter. Net interest income decreased only by 0.3% Q/Q and by 2.8% YoY despite lower interest rates. Probably, UniCredit's customers are as happy as investors about these results, because it means UniCredit slowed the pass-through process. The cost-income ratio was below 37.8%, which makes UniCredit a leader to this extent. The CET1 ratio was 16.2%.

Now, overall, the results were good, thanks to UniCredit's balanced mix of revenue streams, which is made up of NII as well as fees, loans, and deposits. However, analysts and investors were waiting for the earnings call to talk with UniCredit's CEO about the withdrawal from the Banco BPM bid, its doubled stake in Greece's Alpha Bank, and its increased stake in Commerzbank (OTCPK:CRZBF) to 28%. When asked about these topics, Mr. Orcel replied in this way:

This quarter is full of noise. Our withdrawal from BPM, the restructuring of our hedges on Commerzbank, the internationalization of life insurance, the one-off risk and charges anticipated in the future, rates coming down, I would say, faster than we anticipated they would come down at the beginning of the year, volatility from Liberation Day, and everything else. But very clearly, the improvement -- the underlying results for Q1 and Q2 are that underlying, and they are stronger than last year as a whole, and in most of the KPI lines.

So, the difficult task of this earnings report was to filter through the noise and focus on the fundamentals. Looking at the numbers we have seen above, we can consider UniCredit's first part of its turnaround completed, with top-notch metrics reached across the board. The second part of the program will focus more on new opportunities and on completing the operational changes still needed.

As a result, when we look at the upgraded guidance and the 2027 ambition, we see a few things that are noteworthy. First of all, UniCredit's distribution remains outstanding. Even though the stock has been a 9x since its Covid-lows, its dividend still yields 5%, with the perspective of another strong increase since UniCredit announced that it will pay in November the interim 2025 dividend of EUR 2.1B (+46% YoY). Secondly, the buyback program was upgraded to EUR 3.6B and will be executed starting this month. So we are talking about a highly rewarding stock that keeps returning billions into the shareholders' pockets while delivering outstanding capital returns.

However, when we look at the 2027 ambition, we can't help but notice that UniCredit expects to keep growing in the next two years, but the pace seems to be slower. Therefore, I think what will support the stock will be the bank's ability to keep returning cash to the shareholders.

Right now, UniCredit trades at a TTM P/B of 1.6 with its fwd P/B estimated to be around 1.3, which helps us understand that investors are expecting more growth from UniCredit. So, looking ahead, I believe the stock's upside doesn't rest on skyrocketing revaluations but more on strategic optionality. Investors seeking the right balance of income and value exposure to Europe's banks should consider UniCredit, as it deserves a place at least on the watchlist. Investors looking for a bank moving at the same accelerated pace we have seen over the past few years should, on the other hand, be a bit more cautious because it seems UniCredit's growth pace will gradually slow down.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Arthur J. Gallagher: A High-Quality Stock That's Worth The Premium
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome William Davis as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. (NYSE:AJG) is a promising investment opportunity. Despite the stock being at a premium, its consistent double-digit growth, strong profitability, and margin expansion suggest that the stock still has room to grow. Furthermore, AJG’s strategic acquisitions enable the company to continue its rapid growth by pursuing external growth opportunities along with its organic growth.

The Company Overview

Insurance Brokerage

While Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. has multiple revenue sources, the heart of the business is its robust insurance brokerage. During the first quarter of 2025, AJG’s brokerage operations generated $3.31 billion in revenue. This was a sizable leap from the first quarter of 2024, where the company generated $2.86 billion. While this rate of growth is impressive, it's even more impressive given that 9.5% of the growth was organic. This highlights the firm's ability to grow its client base and existing relationships without solely relying on acquisitions. The profits from the insurance brokerage have risen dramatically. It far surpassed the $652.6 million earnings from the first quarter of 2024 by gaining $816.1 million in the first quarter of 2025, further entrenching it as the primary engine of the company.

Risk Management

The primary risk management subsidiary of Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. is Gallagher Bassett. In addition to delivering stable performance, this section of the business is increasingly playing a strategic role in the company’s core operations. Over the first part of 2025, it posted revenue of $373.4 million, which represents a 5.8% year-over-year increase. While it is relatively modest compared to the brokerage side of the business, it represents a stable source of income that adds to margins. Also, risk management demand increases during market downturns as businesses seek to protect themselves. This provides the business with revenue and profits that could buoy it through recessions or other periods of uncertainty.

HR Consulting

The third major market for Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. is its HR consulting operations. This portion of the business seeks to assist businesses and business owners with managing human resources and benefits planning. This includes regulatory compliance issues as well as long-term planning for the enterprise. This wing of the company helps to increase its attractiveness to potential clients by allowing the firm to provide a holistic package of services. As a result, this allows the company to build very deep relationships with its clients. Furthermore, it creates opportunities for cross-selling products, which increases the revenue per client.

Growth Strategy

Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. pursues a two-pronged growth strategy. It concentrates on organic growth through internal expansion and cross-selling of its products. However, it also pursues a highly aggressive acquisition model. This approach enables the company to enjoy a far more rapid rate of growth. In the first quarter of 2025 alone, the company acquired 11 companies. Together, these firms contribute almost $100 million in revenue. To further expand AJG’s insurance operations, it acquired Woodruff Sawyer. This was a large deal that immediately added $250 million in recurring revenue.

AJG's Catalysts And Strengths

Arthur J. Gallagher’s growth is bolstered by durable strengths stemming from its acquisition strategy and adoption of AI. Among the most important is its ability to continue pursuing an aggressive acquisition strategy. In the first quarter of 2025, the company went through 11 mergers, which added $100 million in annualized revenue. Furthermore, the company’s acquisition of Woodruff Sawyer has added another $250 million in revenue. This rapid growth comes after 20 consecutive quarters of double-digit growth, which further reinforces the company’s operational efficiency and strategic discipline.

The company’s effective adoption and aggressive plans for AI deployment are another strength. Insurance and risk management companies must assess voluminous amounts of data in order to arrive at profitable premiums and other fees. The use of AI has the potential to streamline this process, which may dramatically increase operational efficiency. As a result, AJG may be positioned to underwrite insurance for lesser served markets or those that are particularly high risk, which has the potential to greatly increase revenue and margins.

Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. has provided double-digit year-over-year growth. From the second quarter of 2023 to the first quarter of 2025, the company’s quarterly revenue has risen from $2.44 billion to $3.08 billion. Much of this growth can be attributed to the company’s aggressive but strategic approach to acquiring new businesses.

Not surprisingly, the company has also enjoyed a surge in net earnings. In the second quarter of 2023, the company recorded net earnings of $235.8 million, which grew to $486.6 million in the first quarter of 2023 before ballooning to $704.4 million in the first quarter of 2025. If you are looking for a company that consistently boosts both its sales and profits, AJG certainly makes a strong argument for itself. Additionally, investors who are worried about its strategy of aggressive acquisitions may be soothed to see that so far the plan is working.

In addition to being focused on growth, Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. is also efficient at leveraging its newly acquired businesses into profitable components. The company has rapidly increased its net earnings margin. From the end of 2024 through the first quarter of 2025, the firm went from a net earnings margin of 13.5% to 23%. During the same period, AJG increased its operating margin from 19.78% to 21.24%. While this growth would be impressive for most companies, it is particularly eye-catching for a company with so much acquisition activity.

The company’s cash and equivalents decreased from $1.55 billion in the first quarter of 2023 to $1.42 billion in 2024. Not surprisingly, this decrease is largely the result of Arthur J. Gallagher & Co.'s acquisitions requiring it to use some of its cash. Additionally, the company has increased its total debt from $6.70 billion to $8.08 billion. Reflecting the company’s acquisition approach, the total assets have swollen from $45.1 billion to $63.0 billion.

While the company’s cash has decreased and its debt has increased, it has been successful in boosting cash flow. From the first quarter of 2023 to the second quarter of 2024, cash flow increased from 428.5 million to $908.8 million. Despite the spending spree, the cash flow is growing faster than the acquisition expenses.

Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. is currently trading at a P/E of 46.61, which is far more than the P/E of its peers. For example, Marsh McLennan (MMC) has a P/E of 25.66, Aon (AON) is at 29.81, and Brown & Brown (BRO) has a value of 29.43. In addition to being far higher than its peers, AJG’s current P/E is also above both its three- and five-year averages. For example, the five-year P/E is 38.84 while the three-year is at 44.08.

In terms of P/S ratio, AJG is also higher than the insurance industry average. Its 6.81 P/S ratio is almost double the industry average. Additionally, its EV/EBITDA ratio is also substantially higher than the industry average. Its ratio of 21.5 far surpasses the industry average of 9.2. This implies that the market places a high premium on Arthur J. Gallagher & Co.’s ability to consistently generate cash flow. While these factors suggest that the stock is overpriced, its much faster rate of growth and higher profitability suggest that it may still have room to rise.

Estimating Arthur J. Gallagher's Valuation

A simplified discounted cash flow analysis can help with assessing the intrinsic value of Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. By using 2024 free cash flow of $2.441 billion as the base and projecting a five-year phase with declining growth rates, it may be possible to gain a more detailed understanding of the value of the company. After this period, a terminal growth rate of 2.5% can be applied. This rate is consistent with mature, cash-generative companies in the insurance sector.

These results suggest that the enterprise value is $77.94 billion. After considering AJG’s $16.7 billion in cash and $13.1 billion in total debt, the equity value came to approximately $81.54 billion. Dividing this by the total shares outstanding yields an intrinsic value of $318.39 per share. Therefore, it appears that AJG is between fairly priced to slightly undervalued.

From a valuation standpoint, upside appears limited above $340 per share, where AJG would trade at more than 25 times forward free cash flow. This is substantially above historical norms. Conversely, a decline towards $285 would offer a more compelling target. However, margin erosion or acquisition integration issues would signal potential weaknesses emerging in the company’s growth strategy, which may indicate a need to exit the position.

Assessing The Risks

The insurance brokerage market is highly competitive and undergoing substantial consolidation. While AJG’s acquisition strategy has driven much of its growth, it also exposes the company to fiercer competition for acquisition targets. More specifically, Marsh McLennan, Aon, and private equity firms aggressively bid on many of the same acquisition opportunities. This creates a risk of driving up the acquisition multiples, which dramatically increases the risk of overpaying for targets. Additionally, the increasingly competitive environment results in rising costs related to marketing, client acquisition, and post-merger employee retention. These costs all represent meaningful pressure on margins.

Acquisitions are central to AJG’s strategy, but it has its own operational and financial risks. Successful integration requires harmonizing company cultures, integrating IT systems, and retaining key personnel under compressed timelines. Failure in any one of these areas can reduce the expected ROI from a deal and drain management focus. Due to this threat, it is imperative for AJG to avoid this by continuing to be strategic in the acquisitions it makes.

Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. is a prime target for cyberattacks due to the large amounts of client data that it holds. Despite insurance companies investing heavily in cyber defenses, the frequency of threats continues to increase across the industry. In 2020, AJG suffered a breach that led to a $21 million class-action settlement affecting over 3 million individuals. Beyond the direct financial consequences of these breaches, they also damage client trust and brand image. Unfortunately, AJG’s acquisition-driven growth model adds complexity to this challenge. Newly acquired firms may bring security vulnerabilities that are difficult to identify and integrate.

Conclusion

Arthur J. Gallagher stands out in the insurance industry. It is among the largest players while also offering a unique combination of attributes for businesses in this space. In particular, it offers a chance to invest in a company that exhibits sustained revenue growth, increasing profit margins, and the defensive characteristics of insurance companies.

The key to AJG’s explosive growth has been its aggressive but thoughtful approach to acquisitions, which have added $350 million in revenue in just a single quarter. Despite its aggressive expansions, the company has continued to keep its margin healthy and minimize friction. While this may be something to keep an eye on, there is currently little evidence that the growth is causing problems for the company.

Due to Arthur J. Gallagher & Co.’s overall health and aggressive growth strategy, the stock should be considered a “Buy” for those looking for a long-term investment. It can also be a viable option for those wanting a stock that can offer stability if an economic downturn were to occur.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation

data.title: Pernod Ricard: Fairly Valued Brand Powerhouse For Dividend Income
data.description:
Pernod Ricard (OTC:PRNDY) is one of the giants in the spirits industry. It names itself as a number one international premium spirits maker with 94 production sites in 24 countries. Its products are distributed in 160 countries. In terms of volume, its best selling brands are Absolut, Jameson and Ballantine’s.

Similarly to other spirit stocks, Pernod Ricard’s shares have more than from the highs of over €200 per native share. Pernod Ricard is maneuvering in the challenging operating environment and protecting its financial position with decisive actions. The shares have fallen to a fair valuation given conservative assumptions. Even though the outlook is unclear, a long-term income investor could take advantage of purchasing shares in an entrenched company with centuries of staying power.

Well-diversified house of brands

Pernod Ricard’s history dates back to 1805 with the establishment of Pernod. By the early merger with Ricard and as a result of a series of acquisitions over the years, the company consists of over 200 brands, including some of the most popular alcohol drinks and balanced across the spectrum of different types of spirits. Its annual revenue is approximately €11-12 billion. The members of the founding Ricard family still have a significant ownership (15%) in the company and Alexandre Ricard is the acting CEO.

Pernod Ricard follows an irregular financial year. In February, Pernod Ricard reported its H1 2025 results. The organic revenue declined 4% and EPS decreased 11%. However, profit from recurring operations declined only by 2%. The company signals the year to be transitory. Fortunately, free cash flow developed well to €440 million, growing 46%. The negative development was rather even in different regions.

In April, Pernod Ricard reported third-quarter results. The organic revenue declined 3%. In H1 it had a negative €110 million impact on its profit from recurring operations and now expects a similar impact in the latter part of the year. The revenue has suffered the most in Europe and global travel retail. In China the Q3 decline was much less than year to date (-5% vs. -22%).

Maneuvering the stormy waters

Spirit companies are maneuvering against several external headwinds. There’s a question of decreasing alcohol consumption in the developed markets partly offset by the trend of premiumization. The industry has gone through phases of overstocking and destocking clouding the financials with swinging revenue. Decreasing purchasing power has led consumers to decrease consumption and choose more affordable brands. Finally, tariffs by China and the U.S. are potentially weakening the position of European brands in the most important alcohol markets.

Alcohol appears to be one of the levers that politicians are pulling in the ongoing trade war. Currently, Pernod Ricard is expecting an annual cost of €200 million, because of the tariffs in China and the U.S. Interpreting results in the coming quarters will likely be complex, if the U.S. customers have been buying a lot before the tariffs and exchange rates distort the figures. The impact of U.S. tariffs are likely to impact the company later in the near future.

China recently imposed a 34.9% levy on European brandy, which was at least an intermediate resolution to an unclear situation. Companies that commit to minimum pricing agreements could be exempt from the duty, this is what Pernod will do. Overall in China, sales of cognac fell 29%, but premium cognac fared better with sales falling 8% last year. The sales of Martell have declined sharply, but brands such as Absolut, Olmeca and Jameson are growing.

Fortunately, Pernod Ricard is widely diversified geographically. India has become the second biggest market for Pernod Ricard before China (12% vs. 9% of revenue) by growing fast. While sales in China declined by 25%, revenue in India grew 6% in H1. India is the second largest volume market for spirits and the largest market for whiskey, which is the number one category for Pernod Ricard.

Pernod Ricard has done several significant actions to mitigate the headwinds. One of them is portfolio optimization. In 2024, it sold a group of wine operations and ten brands for an undisclosed sum. Wines represented around 4% of its revenue and the company retained some of its premium wines and champagnes in its portfolio. The same year it also sold a couple of local brands in Finland and it closed divestments of Clan Campbell and Becherovka brands. And now it is about to sell its Indian whisky brand, Imperial Blue, for €413 million in order to focus on more profitable and fast growing premium brands.

According to Reuters, the company has recently launched a restructuring program in which it is about to organise its brands into two units aiming to cut costs by one billion euros by 2029. The same goes for other spirit producers. Pernod Ricard believes it is gaining market share in the premium segment in most of its operations around the world.

In order to counter the potential long-term adverse trends, Pernod Ricard is active in the ready-to-drink category (RTD). It is a way to get to the hearts and minds of younger generations, which are often considered to consume less alcohol than the ones before. In H1, the RTD category grew 15%. Like other alcohol producers, Pernod Ricard is also exploring opportunities in the THC-infused drinks. It is rumoured to be interested in Brez

Pernod Ricard’s net debt to EBITDA ratio stands rather high at 3.5x. It has a debt load of €12 billion to take care of. The debt has steadily risen from €7.4 billion in FY2021. Most of its debt is fixed-rate and nominated in euros. Approximately half of its debt is due after more than five years. Many of its bonds maturing in the coming years are issued at low interest rates (1-2%), meaning interest expenses are likely to increase in the near future.

Not expensive but not screamingly cheap either

The company updated its outlook for the year in April. It expects low single-digit organic sales decline but steady operating margin. It pursues to protect the cash flow. Analysts are estimating revenue to fall 5.3% and earnings per share to decline 11%. As the revenue, costs, tariffs and foreign exchange rates are all in motion, it is only possible to make a best guess and preferably a range of outcomes, where the earnings are going. The company also reports adjusted numbers and analyst estimates reflect those.

Pernod Ricard is currently trading at a forward P/E-multiple of 14x and EV/EBITDA of 11.7x. Historically, the shares have traded at P/E-multiple of 20-21x and EV/EBITDA of 15x. Pernod Ricard’s earnings per share have grown at 4% CAGR for the past decade, which would not justify historical valuation level. Neither does its average return on capital.

When estimating a fair value for the shares, let’s use FY 2026 analyst EPS estimate as a starting point to mark it as an earnings through. Then we estimate them to recover at a historical rate, apply a terminal multiple of 18x and a discount rate of 10%. This would give us a fair value of €94, slightly below today’s share price of €97. Using a 4% growth rate for the full 10 years or a terminal multiple of 20x, would give us a fair value of €100.

In relation to its competitors, Pernod Ricard (RI) is trading at approximately similar valuation depending on the metric used. Its historical performance is not far off from the rest either. There’s at least one conclusion to be drawn from a peer comparison. A prudent investor looking for a decent safety margin, rather applies a historically lower terminal multiple as done in the fair value calculation above.

Pernod Ricard is followed by 20 analysts with an average target price of €108.

Dividend yield is attractive

Pernod Ricard pays a dividend twice per year. Last year the total dividend was €4.7. The company has announced the first dividend payment of €2.35 with an ex-date 23rd of July. If the second instalment, usually paid in November, follows the pattern of two previous years, the total dividend will be of a similar size than last year. The payout would result in a dividend yield of 4.8%.

From the perspective of the free cash flow, the dividend payment appears stretched. The dividend payout totals €1.2 billion annually. Pernod Ricard’s free cash flow has been approximately €1.3 billion on average. Last year the free cash flow declined 33% to €963 million. However, the capital expenditures and investments in inventory were record high in FY2023 and FY2024, the company expects lower spending and tight cost control to boost cash flow.

Conclusion

Pernod Ricard will likely come out stronger from the challenging operational environment in the spirits industry. Its powerful brands and geographical and product diversification are the company’s backbone. Pernod Ricard has been and is taking decisive actions to prune its portfolio and protect its profitability. The outlook is unclear, to say the least, but the valuation has reached a fair level and shareholder returns are supported by a juicy dividend.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
